title: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval
exclusions: Does NOT verify the root cause of the emergency; focuses on the governance loop only.
evidence: Saved search 'breakglass_post_use_review' runs every 30 min; writes per-incident review status to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AC-6(7) requires review of privileges; break-glass use demands post-hoc approval by design.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AC-6 (Least privilege) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A.5.15 access control — emergency access procedures require review and sign-off.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.15 (Access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Privileged access must be timely reviewed — break-glass is the exception that most frequently fails an ITGC review.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOX ITGC ITGC.AccessMgmt.Privileged (Privileged access) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.40.2: Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.40.2' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a break-glass account is used but no matching approval record exists 48 hours after session close, the UC fires naming the account, session_id, and initiator.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Usages with an approval record and sign-off by the designated reviewer do NOT fire.

spl:
index=pam breakglass=true earliest=-14d
| stats min(_time) as started max(_time) as ended values(session_id) as session_id by account initiator
| join account session_id [search index=grc sourcetype=breakglass:approval | stats values(approver) as approver max(_time) as approved_at by account session_id]
| where isnull(approved_at) AND (now()-ended)>172800
| table account initiator started ended session_id approved_at
description: Every break-glass activation must be followed by a reviewer sign-off within 48 hours. The UC surfaces any usage that escaped the governance loop.
value: Break-glass misuse is the most audited sub-case of privileged access; a missing approval is a textbook SOX-ITGC deficiency.
implementation: (1) Tag break-glass accounts at vault time; (2) emit approval events from the GRC tool; (3) schedule every 30 min; (4) on miss, notify CISO and engage SOAR.
visualization: Table of break-glass activations with approval status, bar chart of usages per quarter, single-value 'activations with 48h approval'.
knownFalsePositives: Approval events tagged against the wrong session_id will look like misses; maintain strict id-matching contracts.
grandmaExplanation: Break-glass misuse is the most audited sub-case of privileged access; a missing approval is a textbook -ITGC deficiency — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: See subcategory preamble..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Tag break-glass accounts at vault time; (2) emit approval events from the GRC tool; (3) schedule every 30 min; (4) on miss, notify CISO and engage SOAR.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=pam breakglass=true earliest=-14d
| stats min(_time) as started max(_time) as ended values(session_id) as session_id by account initiator
| join account session_id [search index=grc sourcetype=breakglass:approval | stats values(approver) as approver max(_time) as approved_at by account session_id]
| where isnull(approved_at) AND (now()-ended)>172800
| table account initiator started ended session_id approved_at
```

Understanding this SPL

**Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval** — Break-glass misuse is the most audited sub-case of privileged access; a missing approval is a textbook SOX-ITGC deficiency.

Documented **Data sources**: See subcategory preamble. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: pam.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=pam, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by account initiator** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(approved_at) AND (now()-ended)>172800` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Break-glass account usage review with mandatory post-use approval**): table account initiator started ended session_id approved_at


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table of break-glass activations with approval status, bar chart of usages per quarter, single-value 'activations with 48h approval'.
